note_en:
Forget the Marvel Cinematic Universe. The crossover event of the year is here, and it’s Google/Facebook.
 A lawsuit  filed today by a coalition of state attorneys general, led by Texas’ Ken Paxton, accuses Google of making an “unlawful agreement” that gave Facebook special privileges in exchange for promising not to support a competing ad system. It’s just one of many claims made in a case that takes broad aim at Google’s  monopoly over the online advertising ecosystem, but it could very well be the most consequential. The case is a civil suit, and it names only Google as a defendant. But if what Texas is alleging is true, then both companies may have violated federal antitrust law—and committed felonies in the process.
 As described in the complaint, the scheme between Google and Facebook has its roots in 2017, when Facebook  announced it would start supporting something called “header bidding.” The details are too wonky to get into here. Basically, Google, which runs the biggest online ad exchange, likes to make publishers give it first dibs on bidding to place an ad. (“Publisher” just means any website or app that runs ads.) Header bidding was a technical hack that allowed publishers to earn higher prices by soliciting bids from multiple exchanges at once. Google hated this, because it created more competition. When Facebook declared that it would work with publishers that used header bidding, it was seen as a provocation. The millions of businesses that advertise with Facebook don’t just advertise  on Facebook; through the Facebook Audience Network, the company also places ads across the web, making it one of the biggest ad buyers on the internet. If it began supporting header bidding, that could cause Google’s ad platform to lose a lot of business.
  Drawing on internal documents uncovered during its investigation, however, the Texas attorney general claims that Facebook’s leaders didn’t actually want to compete with Google; they wanted Google to buy them off. This seems to have worked. In September 2018, the companies cut a deal. Facebook, the complaint says, agreed to “curtail its header bidding initiatives” and send the millions of advertisers in its Facebook Audience Network to bid on Google’s platform. In return, Google would give the Facebook Audience Network special advantages in ad auctions, including setting aside a quota of ad placements to Facebook, even when the company didn’t make the highest bid. The agreement, the complaint says, “fixes prices and allocates markets between Google and Facebook.”
 Here’s why that matters: The other antitrust cases filed against Google and Facebook this year— by the Justice Department for Google and  the Federal Trade Commission and state AGs for Facebook—are based on Section 2 of the Sherman Act, which is about building a monopoly. In a Section 2 case, it isn’t enough to show that a company dominates a market; the government must also prove that it got to the top by using anticompetitive tactics rather than by just being the best.
  The alleged conspiracy between Google and Facebook is different. It falls under Section 1 of the Sherman Act, which makes it illegal for two or more companies to make any contract or agreement “in restraint of trade.” (While the Texas case is a civil suit, the claims in it could conceivably serve as the basis of federal criminal charges.) A Section 1 case is much simpler. If there’s proof that the companies did agree to fix prices, rig bids, or just not compete with each other, that’s the end of the inquiry.
  “If you can prove an agreement between two firms, once you have proof of that agreement, it is called per se illegal,” said Sally Hubbard, director of enforcement strategy at the Open Markets Institute, an anti-monopoly think tank. “This is why antitrust enforcers love to bring Section 1 cases, because if you can find evidence of more than one firm agreeing to fix prices, agreeing not to compete, agreeing to allocate a market—once you prove that agreement, it’s automatically illegal. It ends there.”
  Google says there’s nothing improper about the arrangement. A company spokesperson said that Facebook is one of more than 25 companies that participate in Google’s Open Bidding program and doesn’t receive any special privileges.
 Does Texas have evidence to the contrary? It’s tough to tell, because the complaint is maddeningly redacted. Unlike last week’s Facebook antitrust filing or the  House report on Big Tech from over the summer, most of the juicy details and internal documents, including a screenshot of the contract terms between Facebook and Google, are blacked out. (The heavy redactions are not the only strange thing about the case. The Texas attorney general’s office, fresh off a losing effort to overturn the presidential election, announced the suit in a weird, amateurish  video posted to Twitter before the case had been filed.) Still, there are some tantalizing clues. The document alludes to an email about the arrangement from Dan Rose, Facebook’s vice president of partnerships, to CEO Mark Zuckerberg. That suggests that whatever deal the two companies had was signed off on at the highest levels. Another heavily redacted section alleges Google violated users’ privacy in “egregious ways” after signing an agreement with Facebook in 2015 that gave the company access to "millions of Americans’ end-to-end encrypted WhatsApp messages, photos, videos, and audio files." (Facebook  acquired WhatsApp the year before.)
 Again, the Facebook arrangement is just one part of the Texas case. More broadly, the complaint accuses Google of taking advantage of its control over each step of the online advertising chain—from the tool businesses use to place ads, to the platform publishers use to make their ad space available, to the exchange where the two sides meet—in ways that stifle competition and pad Google’s bottom line.
 Google strongly disputes the allegations. “Attorney General Paxton’s ad tech claims are meritless, yet he’s gone ahead in spite of all the facts,” a spokesperson wrote in an emailed statement. “We’ve invested in state-of-the-art ad tech services that help businesses and benefit consumers. Digital ad prices have fallen over the last decade. Ad tech fees are falling too. Google’s ad tech fees are lower than the industry average. These are the hallmarks of a highly competitive industry. We will strongly defend ourselves from his baseless claims in court.” (Facebook did not immediately respond to a request for comment.)
 Google, in other words, is arguing that its role in online advertising has been good for everyone. That’s a great argument to make—for the parts of the case brought under Section 2. If Google and Facebook did make a deal to back off of direct competition—which, again, Google denies—none of those other factors will save the company from Section 1. Ever since the first rumblings of an antitrust movement against Big Tech began, the dominant companies have insisted that the sector is competitive, relying on their competitors’ existence to rebut the claim that they’re too big or powerful. Google may turn out to have relied on its competitors a little too much.

title: 看看德克萨斯州反托拉斯诉讼中的指控，指责谷歌为Facebook提供特殊特权，以换取不支持竞争性广告系统的回报
title_en: A look at allegations in Texas AG's antitrust suit, accusing Google of giving Facebook special privileges in exchange for not supporting a competing ad system

note:
忘了漫威电影宇宙。今年的跨界活动就在这里，它是Google / Facebook。
 由德克萨斯州的肯·帕克斯顿（Ken Paxton）领导的州检察长联合会今天提起诉讼，指责谷歌做出“非法协议”，给予Facebook特别特权，以换取承诺不支持竞争性广告系统。这只是针对Google垄断在线广告生态系统而提出的众多主张之一，但这很可能是最重要的。此案属于民事诉讼，仅以Google为被告。但是，如果得克萨斯州的说法是正确的，那么两家公司都可能违反了联邦反托拉斯法，并在此过程中犯了重罪。
 如投诉所述，谷歌和Facebook之间的计划始于2017年，当时Facebook宣布将开始支持所谓的“标头竞标”。细节太笨拙了，无法进入这里。基本上，运行最大的在线广告交易平台的Google希望使发布商在竞标时优先考虑投放广告。 （“发布者”仅指任何运行广告的网站或应用。）标头出价是一种技术性手段，允许发布者通过一次从多个交易所征集出价来获得更高的价格。谷歌讨厌这个，因为它创造了更多的竞争。当Facebook宣布它将与使用标头出价的发布商合作时，这被视为一种挑衅。数以百万计的使用Facebook做广告的企业不仅仅在Facebook上做广告；通过Facebook Audience Network，该公司还将广告投放到整个网络上，使其成为互联网上最大的广告购买者之一。如果它开始支持标头出价，则可能导致Google的广告平台失去很多业务。
  得克萨斯州总检察长利用调查期间发现的内部文件，声称Facebook的领导人实际上并不想与Google竞争。他们希望Google买断它们。这似乎奏效了。在2018年9月，两家公司达成了交易。诉状称，Facebook同意“缩减其标头竞标计划”，并在其Facebook受众网络中将数百万广告商送往谷歌平台竞标。作为回报，谷歌将在广告拍卖中为Facebook受众网络提供特殊的优势，包括为Facebook预留一定的广告展示位置，即使该公司的出价最高。投诉称，该协议“确定价格，并在Google和Facebook之间分配市场。”
 这就是为什么这样重要：今年，由Google司法部和联邦贸易委员会以及针对Facebook的州立股份公司针对Google和Facebook提起的其他反托拉斯案件均基于《谢尔曼法》第2节，即关于建立垄断的规定。在第2节的案例中，还不足以表明一家公司在市场上占主导地位；政府还必须通过使用反竞争策略而不是仅仅做到最好来证明自己达到了顶峰。
  谷歌和Facebook之间的所谓阴谋是不同的。它属于《谢尔曼法》第1条的规定，该条规定两个或两个以上的公司订立任何“限制贸易”合同或协议是非法的。 （虽然德克萨斯州的案件是民事诉讼，但可以认为其中的主张可以作为联邦刑事指控的依据。）第1节案件要简单得多。如果有证据表明两家公司确实同意确定价格，钻机出价，或者只是彼此不竞争，那么调查就此结束了。
  “如果能够证明两家公司之间的协议，一旦有了该协议的证明，就其本身而言是非法的，”反垄断智囊机构开放市场研究所执行策略主任萨利·哈伯德说。 “这就是为什么反托拉斯执法人员喜欢提出第一节案的原因，因为如果您能找到多家公司同意定价，同意不参与竞争，同意分配市场的证据，一旦您证明该协议，即自动成为非法。到此为止。” 
Google表示，这项安排没有任何不当之处。公司发言人说，Facebook是参与Google Open Bidding计划的25多家公司之一，并且没有获得任何特殊特权。
 德州有相反的证据吗？很难说出来，因为投诉被疯狂地删除了。与上周夏天的Facebook反托拉斯文件或众议院关于大科技的报告不同，夏季的大部分多汁细节和内部文件（包括Facebook和Google之间的合同条款屏幕截图）都被涂黑了。 （繁琐的修改并非唯一奇怪的事情。得克萨斯州检察长办公室刚刚结束了推翻总统大选的失败努力，在提起诉讼之前在Twitter上发布了一个奇怪的业余视频，宣布了该诉讼。 ）不过，仍有一些诱人的线索。该文件暗示了一封电子邮件，内容涉及从Facebook合作伙伴副总裁Dan Rose到CEO Mark Zuckerberg的安排。这表明，两家公司达成的任何协议均已在最高级别上签署。另一个经过大量修改的部分指控Google在2015年与Facebook签署协议后以“令人震惊的方式”侵犯了用户的隐私，该协议使该公司可以访问数以百万计的美国人端到端加密的WhatsApp消息，照片，视频，和音频文件。＆＃34; （Facebook在一年前收购了WhatsApp。）
 同样，Facebook的安排只是德克萨斯案的一部分。从更广泛的角度来看，该投诉指责谷歌利用其对在线广告链各个步骤的控制权-从企业用来放置广告的工具，到发布商用来提供广告空间的平台，以及双方之间的交流。交流，以扼杀竞争并巩固Google的底线。
 Google强烈反对这些指控。一位发言人在一封电子邮件声明中写道：“总检察长帕克斯顿的广告技术主张毫无根据，尽管如此，他还是取得了进步。” “我们已经投资了最先进的广告技术服务，这些服务可以帮助企业和消费者受益。在过去十年中，数字广告价格已经下降。广告技术费用也在下降。 Google的广告技术费用低于行业平均水平。这些是竞争激烈的行业的标志。我们将坚决捍卫他在法庭上毫无根据的主张。” （Facebook没有立即回应置评请求。）
 换句话说，谷歌正在争论其在在线广告中的作用对所有人都有好处。对于第2节中提出的部分案件，这是一个很好的论点。如果Google和Facebook确实达成了避免直接竞争的协议（再次遭到Google的否认），那么其他任何因素都不会使公司免于竞争。第1节。自从针对大科技公司发起反托拉斯运动的第一声谣言开始，主导公司就一直坚持认为该行业具有竞争优势，依靠其竞争对手的存在来驳斥其规模太大或实力太强的说法。谷歌可能证明过分依赖竞争对手。
 美国东部时间12月16-20日下午7:33更新：此故事已更新，并附有Google的其他评论。

note_en:
Forget the Marvel Cinematic Universe. The crossover event of the year is here, and it’s Google/Facebook.
 A lawsuit  filed today by a coalition of state attorneys general, led by Texas’ Ken Paxton, accuses Google of making an “unlawful agreement” that gave Facebook special privileges in exchange for promising not to support a competing ad system. It’s just one of many claims made in a case that takes broad aim at Google’s  monopoly over the online advertising ecosystem, but it could very well be the most consequential. The case is a civil suit, and it names only Google as a defendant. But if what Texas is alleging is true, then both companies may have violated federal antitrust law—and committed felonies in the process.
 As described in the complaint, the scheme between Google and Facebook has its roots in 2017, when Facebook  announced it would start supporting something called “header bidding.” The details are too wonky to get into here. Basically, Google, which runs the biggest online ad exchange, likes to make publishers give it first dibs on bidding to place an ad. (“Publisher” just means any website or app that runs ads.) Header bidding was a technical hack that allowed publishers to earn higher prices by soliciting bids from multiple exchanges at once. Google hated this, because it created more competition. When Facebook declared that it would work with publishers that used header bidding, it was seen as a provocation. The millions of businesses that advertise with Facebook don’t just advertise  on Facebook; through the Facebook Audience Network, the company also places ads across the web, making it one of the biggest ad buyers on the internet. If it began supporting header bidding, that could cause Google’s ad platform to lose a lot of business.
  Drawing on internal documents uncovered during its investigation, however, the Texas attorney general claims that Facebook’s leaders didn’t actually want to compete with Google; they wanted Google to buy them off. This seems to have worked. In September 2018, the companies cut a deal. Facebook, the complaint says, agreed to “curtail its header bidding initiatives” and send the millions of advertisers in its Facebook Audience Network to bid on Google’s platform. In return, Google would give the Facebook Audience Network special advantages in ad auctions, including setting aside a quota of ad placements to Facebook, even when the company didn’t make the highest bid. The agreement, the complaint says, “fixes prices and allocates markets between Google and Facebook.”
 Here’s why that matters: The other antitrust cases filed against Google and Facebook this year— by the Justice Department for Google and  the Federal Trade Commission and state AGs for Facebook—are based on Section 2 of the Sherman Act, which is about building a monopoly. In a Section 2 case, it isn’t enough to show that a company dominates a market; the government must also prove that it got to the top by using anticompetitive tactics rather than by just being the best.
  The alleged conspiracy between Google and Facebook is different. It falls under Section 1 of the Sherman Act, which makes it illegal for two or more companies to make any contract or agreement “in restraint of trade.” (While the Texas case is a civil suit, the claims in it could conceivably serve as the basis of federal criminal charges.) A Section 1 case is much simpler. If there’s proof that the companies did agree to fix prices, rig bids, or just not compete with each other, that’s the end of the inquiry.
  “If you can prove an agreement between two firms, once you have proof of that agreement, it is called per se illegal,” said Sally Hubbard, director of enforcement strategy at the Open Markets Institute, an anti-monopoly think tank. “This is why antitrust enforcers love to bring Section 1 cases, because if you can find evidence of more than one firm agreeing to fix prices, agreeing not to compete, agreeing to allocate a market—once you prove that agreement, it’s automatically illegal. It ends there.”
  Google says there’s nothing improper about the arrangement. A company spokesperson said that Facebook is one of more than 25 companies that participate in Google’s Open Bidding program and doesn’t receive any special privileges.
 Does Texas have evidence to the contrary? It’s tough to tell, because the complaint is maddeningly redacted. Unlike last week’s Facebook antitrust filing or the  House report on Big Tech from over the summer, most of the juicy details and internal documents, including a screenshot of the contract terms between Facebook and Google, are blacked out. (The heavy redactions are not the only strange thing about the case. The Texas attorney general’s office, fresh off a losing effort to overturn the presidential election, announced the suit in a weird, amateurish  video posted to Twitter before the case had been filed.) Still, there are some tantalizing clues. The document alludes to an email about the arrangement from Dan Rose, Facebook’s vice president of partnerships, to CEO Mark Zuckerberg. That suggests that whatever deal the two companies had was signed off on at the highest levels. Another heavily redacted section alleges Google violated users’ privacy in “egregious ways” after signing an agreement with Facebook in 2015 that gave the company access to "millions of Americans’ end-to-end encrypted WhatsApp messages, photos, videos, and audio files." (Facebook  acquired WhatsApp the year before.)
 Again, the Facebook arrangement is just one part of the Texas case. More broadly, the complaint accuses Google of taking advantage of its control over each step of the online advertising chain—from the tool businesses use to place ads, to the platform publishers use to make their ad space available, to the exchange where the two sides meet—in ways that stifle competition and pad Google’s bottom line.
 Google strongly disputes the allegations. “Attorney General Paxton’s ad tech claims are meritless, yet he’s gone ahead in spite of all the facts,” a spokesperson wrote in an emailed statement. “We’ve invested in state-of-the-art ad tech services that help businesses and benefit consumers. Digital ad prices have fallen over the last decade. Ad tech fees are falling too. Google’s ad tech fees are lower than the industry average. These are the hallmarks of a highly competitive industry. We will strongly defend ourselves from his baseless claims in court.” (Facebook did not immediately respond to a request for comment.)
 Google, in other words, is arguing that its role in online advertising has been good for everyone. That’s a great argument to make—for the parts of the case brought under Section 2. If Google and Facebook did make a deal to back off of direct competition—which, again, Google denies—none of those other factors will save the company from Section 1. Ever since the first rumblings of an antitrust movement against Big Tech began, the dominant companies have insisted that the sector is competitive, relying on their competitors’ existence to rebut the claim that they’re too big or powerful. Google may turn out to have relied on its competitors a little too much.

note:
“是火车！”瑞安·霍利斯特（Ryan Hollister）闯入他们在加利福尼亚州特洛克（Turlock）的家中向妻子劳拉（Laura）大喊大叫。在2017年的两个星期中，他们一直在盯着新安装的Raspberry Shake（Raspberry Pi驱动的仪器）中的数据，该仪器可以检测地面在特定位置的移动方式。他们期望看到遥远地震的震撼，却以固定的间隔看到了奇特的雪茄形波形。劳拉·霍利斯特（Laura Hollister）说：“最大的挑战就是噪音。”
 赖安·霍利斯特（Ryan Hollister）说：“我以为是厕所冲水或洗衣机，”但是对上厕所或洗衣服的简单测试证明他是错的。当他困在车上看着火车经过Turlock的嘎嘎作响时，他意识到纵横交错的这条小径可能会造成这种神秘的地震噪音。他一回到家，就获取了Raspberry Shake的数据。果然，每道怪异的强烈地震波都对应于一列火车，振幅最大的波与最近的赛道时间表相关，离家只有半英里。
  这不是他们的地震监听设备最后一次拾取到人类活动的迹象。当COVID-19吞噬我们的世界时，地球科学教育家的丈夫和妻子团队Hollisters注意到他们的Raspberry Shake的活动水平比平时低得多。当他们的街道（当地高中的主要动脉）本来应该吸引青少年时，这种下降就明显了。
 这种变化远不止于特洛克。地震学家托马斯·莱科克（Thomas Lecocq）特别关注地球的普遍振动，他在比利时皇家天文台的职权范围内，发现一个永久地震台站的高频噪声明显降低。这种奇特的安静程度比他在圣诞节和新年之间的低迷时期见过的安静且更长，并且恰逢他的国家被禁闭。
    在接下来的几个月中，来自世界各地的Lecocq和76位合著者使用Lecocq为此专门编写的Python代码，梳理了来自70多个国家/地区的地震台站的数据。共有268个台站具有可用数据，其中185个台站的高频地震噪声在城市地区骤然下降了50％。随着每个国家/地区针对COVID-19的关闭，这些更改也步调一致。 Lecocq的合著者之一伊恩·内斯比特（Ian Nesbitt）说，随着来自驾驶，建筑甚至是步行的信号消失，“我们也许能够研究以前看不见的[地质]信号，因为该信号被该噪音掩盖了。”
 许多台站是大学或政府科学家安装的高端研究仪器。但是有65个很小的覆盆子奶昔，坐在科学家和业余爱好者的房屋和办公室中。事实证明，当人们发出巨大的噪音时，令人不安的是，任何拥有备用Raspberry Pi和数百美元的Raspberry Shake电路板以及一些传感器的人都可以看到它。
  地震台网负责人Emily Wolin说，地震台站的基本配方需要四个要素：用于测量地球运动的传感器，用于记录测量值的手段，长期存储解决方案（本地或其他地方）以及电源。美国地质调查局（USGS）阿尔伯克基地震实验室。 
先进的地震台拥有众多传感器，它们可以检测巨大范围的频率，并捕获地球在上下，西北和南北三个方向上的运动。数字化仪和数据记录仪可精确记录数据并为其添加时间戳。沃林说，为设备供电时，最偏远的站点可能会使用太阳能电池板，其功率要求会根据通信需求而变化很大。
  沃林说，要将一个新的地震台站添加到地震监测网络中，科学家们必须搜索考虑了区域地质和可能的噪声源的位置，例如铁路（霍利斯特的家永远都不会被砍掉）。然后，借助候选站点列表，他们确定并联系土地所有者以获取许可，并确保访问权限以进行施工，安装和后续维护。
    沃林解释说，有时，准备工作可能涉及“雇用钻机在坚固的岩石上钻数百米”。在某些情况下，必须精心构造热密封防水地震保险库，以容纳设备，使它们如此敏感，否则它们将承受压力和温度的微小变化。避难所还有助于最大程度地减少讨厌的人为噪声。 USGS地震学家Sue Hough说，要安装传感器和电子设备，“这不是火箭科学，但确实需要接受特殊培训。”
 复杂性的每一层都增加了另一条线。根据霍夫的说法，地震台的顶级版本的成本可能超过10,000美元，这不包括安装成本。 Raspberry Shake的首席执行官布兰登·克里斯滕森（Branden Christensen）表示，如果将这些费用包括在内，则安装一个地震台站的成本可能高达100,000美元。这些价格是政府机构，研究机构和行业完全可以负担​​的。
 另一方面，Raspberry Shakes具有相同组件的基本版本，而价格却很少。 Raspberry Shake电路板的价格仅为100美元，几乎可以插入任何具有以太网或无线功能的Raspberry Pi。 Christensen说：“我们认为人们会把[Raspberry Pis]坐在他们的抽屉里，并且[我们设计Raspberry Shakes来]支持所有人。”
 地震传感器（如地震检波器）插入Raspberry Shake板中，该板用作放大器和数字化仪。传感器的输出采用电压差的形式，必须将其放大并转换为每速度已知的电压。也是树莓摇床的前首席科学家的内斯比特（Nesbitt）称，这种转换称为增益，使输出保持电压单位。
 Raspberry Shake对这些信息进行数字化处理，然后将其通过管道传送到Raspberry Pi，以进行进一步的处理和存档。 Nesbitt将其描述为Raspberry Pi的硬盘驱动器，容量为8 GB，它随Raspberry Shake一起提供，并预装有所有Shake软件。 Raspberry Pi装有SD卡，并为整个地震台供电。 “ [Raspberry Pi]是构成一切的基础计算机，” Nesbitt说。 
使用Raspberry Shake板，从头开始构建您自己的地震台变得像添加传感器并将Raspberry Pi插入墙壁插座一样简单，尽管Christensen建议制作一个外壳（您可以使用Lego积木！）来保护它免受颠簸。 的家庭居民。  如果您不想从头开始组装自己的产品，Raspberry Shake会根据您想要的传感器的数量和类型提供几个总包选项。 霍夫说，交钥匙选项将所有这些组件包装到一个紧凑的有机玻璃盒子中。  Hollisters选择了交钥匙的Raspberry Shake 4D，价格不到400美元。 要进行安装，Ryan Hollister说，他们需要做的就是“将其水平并且将轴指向正确的方向，以便正确定向，然后将其插入。” 容易，那么……pi（e）。
note_en:
“It’s the trains!” Ryan Hollister yelled to his wife Laura as he burst into their home in Turlock California. For two weeks in 2017, they’d been staring at data from their newly installed Raspberry Shake, a Raspberry Pi-powered instrument that detects how the ground moves at a specific location. Expecting to see the tell-tale wiggles of distant earthquakes, they instead saw peculiar cigar-shaped waveforms at regular intervals. “The biggest challenge,” says Laura Hollister, “was the noise.”
 “I thought it was the toilet flushing or the washing machine,” says Ryan Hollister, but simple tests of going to the restroom or doing the laundry proved him wrong. While stuck in his car watching a train rattle through Turlock, he realized the three tracks that criss-cross this small California town could be causing this mystery seismic noise. As soon as he got home, he pulled up the Raspberry Shake’s data. Sure enough, each weirdly intense caterpillar of seismic waves corresponded to a train, with the highest-amplitude waves correlating with the nearest track’s schedule, only a half mile from home.
  It wasn’t the last time that their seismic listening device picked up signs of human activity. As COVID-19 engulfed our world, the Hollisters, a husband-wife team of Earth science educators, noticed that their Raspberry Shake registered much lower levels of activity than usual. The drop was pronounced at times when their street, a main artery to the local high school, should have been pulsing with teenagers.
 That change was far from limited to Turlock. Thomas Lecocq, a seismologist who pays particular attention to Earth’s ubiquitous vibrations, discerned a marked decrease in high frequency noise on a permanent seismic station  under his purview at the Royal Observatory of Belgium. This peculiar hush was quieter and longer than the one he’d seen during the subdued days between Christmas and New Year, and coincided with his country’s lockdown.
    In the following months, Lecocq and 76 coauthors from around the world combed through data from seismic stations spanning more than 70 countries using Python code Lecocq wrote specifically for this purpose. A total of 268 stations had usable data, and 185 of them saw high frequency seismic noise plummet by up to 50 percent in urban regions. The changes came in lockstep with each country’s closure in response to COVID-19. As the signals from driving, construction, and even walking fell away, Ian Nesbitt, one of Lecocq’s coauthors says, “We may be able to investigate [geologic] signals that we previously couldn’t see because it was masked by that noise.”
 Many of the stations were high-end research instruments installed by university or government scientists. But 65 were tiny Raspberry Shakes, sitting in the homes and offices of scientists and hobbyists alike. It turns out that when humans make a lot of noise, seismically speaking, anyone with a spare Raspberry Pi and a few hundred dollars for a Raspberry Shake circuit board and some sensors can see it.
  The basic recipe for a seismic station requires four ingredients: sensors to measure Earth’s motion, a means to record the measurements, a long-term storage solution (either local or elsewhere), and a power source, says Emily Wolin, Seismic Network Manager for the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) Albuquerque Seismological Laboratory.
 State-of-the-art seismic stations  boast numerous sensors that detect an immense range of frequencies, capturing Earth’s movement in three directions—up-down, east-west, and north-south. Digitizers and data loggers precisely record and time stamp the data. To power the equipment, the most remote stations may use solar panels, with power requirements varying dramatically based on communication needs, says Wolin.
  To add a new seismic station to an earthquake monitoring network, Wolin says scientists must scout locations that take into account regional geology and possible noise sources—like railroads (the Hollister’s home would have never made the cut). With a list of candidate sites, they then identify and contact landowners for permission, and secure access for construction, installation, and subsequent maintenance.
    Wolin explains that sometimes, preparation may involve “hiring a drill rig to bore hundreds of meters into solid rock.” In some instances, thermally sealed and waterproof seismic vaults must be carefully constructed to house equipment so sensitive that they would otherwise pick up minuscule changes in pressure and temperature. Vaults also help minimize pesky anthropogenic noise. To install the sensor and electronics, “it’s not rocket science,” says Sue Hough, a USGS seismologist, but “it does take special training.”
 Each layer of complexity adds another line to the bill. According to Hough, top-tier versions of a seismic station can cost well over $10,000, excluding installation costs. Branden Christensen, CEO of Raspberry Shake, says that when those costs are included, installing a single seismic station could cost upwards of $100,000. Those prices are exclusively affordable to government agencies, research institutions, and industry.
 Raspberry Shakes, on the other hand, have basic versions of the same components at a fraction of the price. A Raspberry Shake circuit board costs as little as $100, and it plugs into almost any ethernet or wireless-enabled Raspberry Pi. “We thought that people would have [Raspberry Pis] sitting around in their drawers,” says Christensen, “and we [designed Raspberry Shakes to]  support them all.”
 A seismic sensor, like a geophone, plugs into the Raspberry Shake board, which serves as an amplifier and digitizer. The sensor’s output comes in the form of voltage differences that must be amplified and converted into a known voltage per velocity. This conversion, called a gain, leaves the output in voltage units, according to Nesbitt, who is also Raspberry Shake’s former chief scientist.
 The Raspberry Shake digitizes this information and pipes it to the Raspberry Pi for further processing and archiving. An 8 gigabyte microSD card, which Nesbitt describes as the hard drive of the Raspberry Pi, ships with every Raspberry Shake, and comes pre-loaded with all of the Shake software. The Raspberry Pi houses the SD card and provides power for the entire seismic station. “[The Raspberry Pi] is the computer underlying everything,” says Nesbitt.
 With a Raspberry Shake board,  building your own seismic station from scratch becomes as simple as adding a sensor and plugging the Raspberry Pi into your wall socket, although Christensen recommends crafting an enclosure (you can use  Lego bricks!) to protect it from the bumps of the denizens of your household.
 If you’d rather not assemble your own from scratch, Raspberry Shake makes several turnkey options based on the number and type of sensors you want. Turnkey options, Hough says, pack all these components into a compact plexiglass box.
 The Hollisters chose the turnkey  Raspberry Shake 4D, which can be had for under $400. To install, Ryan Hollister says all they needed to do was, “level it and point the axes in the right direction so it’s oriented properly, and plug it in.” Easy as, well… pi(e).
